source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, AI, global warming, geopolitics
summary: Intel has a real path to recovery under Lip-Bu Tan with US government support, and American-made wafers could command ~100% price premium
summary_extended: Baker argues that Intel has a credible path to recovery under new leadership and with US government backing, making it a potential long opportunity in the semiconductor space. He contends that Pat Gelsinger pursued the only viable strategy for Intel and was unfairly maligned by the press, and that Lip-Bu Tan — a legendary semiconductor investor and executive — understands precisely what needs to be done to execute on the foundry vision. Baker points to TSMC's Arizona fab as concrete evidence of the geopolitical premium thesis: those wafers sold out instantly and TSMC raised prices 20% and still remained sold out, suggesting demand for American-made chips is inelastic and structurally driven by national security fears. He believes these geopolitical fears are real enough that American-made wafers could command close to a 100% price premium over equivalent offshore production, and that the US government taking a stake in Intel signals it is being treated as a national strategic asset. The investment implication is that Intel, if it can execute operationally, is positioned to capture an enormous pricing premium that the market may not be fully pricing in.
detail: Baker argues Pat Gelsinger was unfairly treated by press and embarked on the only viable strategy. Lip-Bu Tan is a legendary semiconductor investor/executive who understands what needs to be done. The US government taking a stake signals Intel is a national asset. TSMC Arizona sold out instantly and raised prices 20% — still sold out. Baker believes geopolitical fears are real enough that American wafers could command close to 100% premium.
confidence_evidence: Baker frames Intel recovery as possible but conditional ('if Intel can just get its act together'), and the 100% wafer premium is stated as a belief rather than a certainty
quote_fragment: you could probably charge close to a 100% premium for American wafers

ticker_audit.reasoning: The thesis is specifically about Intel's recovery path, its foundry strategy, government backing, and its ability to capture the American-wafer price premium.

TSMC Arizona is cited as direct evidence for the American-wafer premium thesis — its instant sellout and 20% price increase validate the structural demand dynamic that also benefits Intel's foundry ambitions. TSM is a direct beneficiary of the reshoring/geopolitical premium theme.

ASML is not mentioned in the context of this specific thesis about Intel's recovery or the American-wafer premium. It likely bled in from an adjacent semiconductor discussion in the same transcript and has no direct role in this thesis.
